section.title: The Bronze Serpent
section.sectionRationale: This chapter turns the corner from forty years of wandering to the first real victories on the way into the land, but at its heart sits one of the Old Testament's most explicitly Christ-shaped scenes: a dying people healed by looking, in faith, at the very image of the judgment God sent against them. Jesus Himself later takes this scene as His own explanation of the cross.
section.thesis: When Israel's impatience turns to open sin against God, He sends venomous snakes among them, yet in the same breath provides healing through a bronze serpent lifted up on a pole, so that anyone who simply looks at it in faith lives, a scene Jesus later applies directly to His own being lifted up on the cross.
section.frameNote: Four movements: victory over Arad and the vow at Hormah; the people's impatience, the snakes, and the bronze serpent lifted up for healing; the journey and the well-song through Moabite territory; and the defeat of Sihon and Og, opening the way east of the Jordan.

verses.text: When the Canaanite king of Arad, who lived in the Negev, heard that Israel was coming along the road to Atharim, he attacked Israel and captured some prisoners.
So Israel made a vow to the LORD: “If You will deliver this people into our hands, we will devote their cities to destruction.”
And the LORD heard Israel's plea and delivered up the Canaanites. Israel devoted them and their cities to destruction; so they named the place Hormah.

units.label: Impatience, snakes, and the bronze serpent

media.alt: A bronze serpent lifted up on a pole against the desert sky
media.caption: Whoever is bitten and looks at the bronze serpent will live.

verses.text: Then they set out from Mount Hor along the route to the Red Sea, in order to bypass the land of Edom. But the people grew impatient on the journey
and spoke against God and against Moses: “Why have you led us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? There is no bread or water, and we detest this wretched food!”
So the LORD sent {{venomous|venomous}} snakes among the people, and many of the Israelites were bitten and died.

Then the people came to Moses and said, “We have sinned by speaking against the LORD and against you. Intercede with the LORD so He will take the snakes away from us.” So Moses interceded for the people.
Then the LORD said to Moses, “Make a fiery serpent and mount it on a pole. When anyone who is bitten looks at it, he will live.”
So Moses made a {{bronzeserpent|bronze snake}} and mounted it on a pole. If anyone who was bitten looked at the bronze snake, he would live.
Then the Israelites set out and camped at Oboth.
They journeyed from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim in the wilderness opposite Moab to the east.
From there they set out and camped in the Valley of Zered.
From there they moved on and camped on the other side of the Arnon, in the wilderness that extends into the Amorite territory. Now the Arnon is the border between the Moabites and the Amorites.
Therefore it is stated in the Book of the Wars of the LORD: “Waheb in Suphah and the wadis of the Arnon,
even the slopes of the wadis that extend to the site of Ar and lie along the border of Moab.”
From there they went on to Beer, the well where the LORD said to Moses, “Gather the people so that I may give them water.”
Then Israel sang this song: “Spring up, O well, all of you sing to it!
The princes dug the well; the nobles of the people hollowed it out with their scepters and with their staffs.” From the wilderness the Israelites went on to Mattanah,
and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth,
and from Bamoth to the valley in Moab where the top of Pisgah overlooks the wasteland.

units.label: Sihon and Og defeated
units.note: the land east of the Jordan taken

verses.text: Then Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites, saying,
“Let us pass through your land. We will not cut through any field or vineyard, or drink water from any well. We will stay on the King's Highway until we have passed through your territory.”
But Sihon would not let Israel pass through his territory. Instead, he gathered his whole army and went out to confront Israel in the wilderness. When he came to Jahaz, he fought against Israel.
And Israel put him to the sword and took possession of his land, from the Arnon to the Jabbok, but only up to the border of the Ammonites, because it was fortified.
Israel captured all the cities of the Amorites and occupied them, including Heshbon and all its villages.
Heshbon was the city of Sihon king of the Amorites, who had fought against the former king of Moab and taken all his land as far as the Arnon.
That is why the poets say: “Come to Heshbon, let it be rebuilt; let the city of Sihon be restored.
For a fire went out from Heshbon, a blaze from the city of Sihon. It consumed Ar of Moab, the rulers of Arnon's heights.
Woe to you, O Moab! You are destroyed, O people of Chemosh! He gave up his sons as refugees, and his daughters into captivity to Sihon king of the Amorites.
But we have overthrown them; Heshbon is destroyed as far as Dibon. We demolished them as far as Nophah, which reaches to Medeba.”
So Israel lived in the land of the Amorites.
After Moses had sent spies to Jazer, Israel captured its villages and drove out the Amorites who were there.
Then they turned and went up the road to Bashan, and Og king of Bashan and his whole army came out to meet them in battle at Edrei.

But the LORD said to Moses, “Do not fear him, for I have delivered him into your hand, along with all his people and his land. Do to him as you did to Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived in Heshbon.”
So they struck down Og, along with his sons and his whole army, until no remnant was left. And they took possession of his land.

venomous.gloss: Fiery or burning serpent, describing the sharp burning pain of the venom. The same root, saraph, later names the seraphim, burning ones, though here it describes the deadly bite God sent as judgment for Israel's contempt of His provision.

bronzeserpent.gloss: Made of bronze, nechoshet, the same word later used for the object's proper name, Nehushtan, in 2 Kings 18:4. The bronze serpent was not magical but a God-appointed sign: healing came not from the object but from looking to it in obedient faith.

basic.context: After forty years, Israel finally begins to make real progress toward the land, winning battles at Hormah and later against Sihon and Og. But in the middle of this progress, the people's old pattern of impatient complaint returns, and God sends venomous snakes as judgment. When the people repent, God provides an unusual remedy: a bronze snake on a pole that anyone bitten could look at and live.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened to a real, dying people in the wilderness, and it is not primarily a story about snakes but about faith: healing came only to those who trusted God enough to look at the very thing shaped like their affliction. Jesus later reads this event as directly about Himself, so the passage carries genuine typological weight, not merely moral lessons.
basic.originalLanguages: The snakes are called nachash saraph, fiery serpents, a burning venom that matches the burning complaint that provoked God's judgment. The remedy God commands is a nechoshet, bronze, image of that very serpent, later called Nehushtan, so that Israel's healing came by looking, in faith, at the image of their own judgment lifted up before them.
basic.typology: Jesus Himself draws the line in John 3:14-15: as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, so that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life. The serpent, symbol of the judgment against sin, is lifted up so the dying can look and live, exactly as Christ, bearing the judgment for sin though sinless Himself, is lifted up on the cross so that all who look to Him in faith are saved.
basic.christ: This is one of the most direct pieces of Old Testament typology in all of Scripture, because Jesus names it Himself. He compares His own crucifixion to this bronze serpent: lifted up publicly, bearing the very form of the judgment against sin, so that everyone who simply looks to Him in faith, rather than earning healing by effort, receives eternal life.
basic.god: God is just, sending real judgment on real rebellion, yet He is also astonishingly gracious, providing a way of healing before the people even finish asking, and making that healing available to anyone who will simply look in faith. His holiness and His mercy meet in the very object of judgment becoming the means of life.
basic.crossReferences: John 3:14-15 is Jesus' own direct application of this scene to His crucifixion. 2 Kings 18:4 records that centuries later King Hezekiah destroyed this same bronze serpent because Israel had begun to worship it, a warning against turning a gift of grace into an idol. 1 Corinthians 10:9 warns against testing Christ as some of the Israelites did in the wilderness.
basic.oneStory: A people under judgment, dying from the very poison of their own rebellion, are healed not by their own effort but by simply looking, in faith, at the sign God provided. That pattern of faith rather than works, looking rather than achieving, runs straight through Scripture to the cross, where Jesus Himself said He would be lifted up exactly as this serpent was, so that everyone who looks to Him in faith might live forever.

context.literary: The chapter alternates between victory and failure, Hormah's triumph, then rebellion and plague, then further victories over Sihon and Og, showing that Israel's forward progress into the land does not mean their old unbelief has disappeared.
context.historical: This episode occurs as Israel finally begins conquering territory east of the Jordan, a hinge point after decades of wandering where military success and spiritual failure sit side by side in the same generation.
context.canonical: The bronze serpent becomes one of Scripture's most important types, later both a genuine relic preserved for centuries and eventually a snare when Israel began worshiping it, until Jesus reclaims and fulfills its true meaning in John 3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show both God's righteous judgment on persistent unbelief and His ready provision of healing for anyone who would trust Him, even in the smallest, strangest-seeming act of looking at a bronze image.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific, unrepeated act of judgment and provision for Israel in the wilderness; it is not prescribing that believers make or look to physical images today. Its principle, that God provides healing to be received by faith rather than earned by effort, is fulfilled in Christ, not repeated in ritual objects.

debates.question: Why did healing come through looking at an image of the very thing that was killing them?

views.label: A picture of confronting sin honestly
views.case: By looking directly at the shape of their judgment, Israel had to acknowledge the seriousness of what their sin had brought upon them, rather than looking away or denying it, before they could be healed.

views.label: A test of simple, trusting obedience
views.case: The healing was not in the object's power but purely in obeying God's stated means; anyone who reasoned it was foolish and refused to look would die, showing that faith, not the mechanism itself, saved them.

debates.bearing: Jesus' own use of this scene in John 3:14-15 leans on both threads: He is lifted up in the very form of judgment against sin, bearing it though sinless, and salvation comes only to those who simply look to Him in trusting faith rather than by their own effort.

study.originalLanguages: The nachash saraph, fiery serpents, brought burning judgment that matched Israel's burning complaint against God. The remedy, a nechoshet, bronze, image of the very serpent that afflicted them, later kept and named Nehushtan, shows that the healing power lay not in the metal but in trusting obedience to God's appointed sign.

typology.type: The bronze serpent lifted up on a pole
typology.fulfillment: Jesus applies this directly to Himself in John 3:14-15: as the serpent was lifted up in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life.

typology.type: Healing given to anyone who simply looked in faith
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates salvation by faith rather than works, since the dying were not asked to perform a cure but only to trust God's word and look, exactly as sinners today are saved by looking to Christ in faith.

study.christ: John 3:14-15 records Jesus' own words: just as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, so that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life. The comparison is exact and startling: the serpent embodied the very judgment against Israel's sin, yet became, when lifted up, the means of life for anyone who looked; Christ, bearing the judgment against all sin though He committed none, becomes, lifted up on the cross, the means of eternal life for anyone who looks to Him in faith.
study.god: God's holiness brings real judgment on persistent rebellion, seen in the venomous snakes sent among the people; yet His mercy responds to the first cry of repentance with a provided way of healing, available freely to anyone who will trust and look. Judgment and grace are not opposites here but appear together in the very same scene.

crossReferences.note: Jesus' own direct application of the bronze serpent to His crucifixion and the life given to all who believe.
Centuries later King Hezekiah destroyed this same bronze serpent, Nehushtan, because Israel had turned it into an idol, a warning against corrupting a sign of grace.
Warns believers not to test Christ as some of the Israelites did in the wilderness, referencing this very generation.
Jesus again speaks of being lifted up, drawing all people to Himself, echoing the same pattern as the serpent lifted up for healing.

study.oneStory: A dying, rebellious people are healed not by their own effort but by simply looking, in faith, at what God provided, even though it took the very shape of their judgment. This pattern, judgment borne and lifted up so that faith alone brings life, runs straight to the cross, where Jesus said He would be lifted up exactly as that serpent was, so that everyone who looks to Him in faith would live forever.

sermon.title: Moses and the Bronze Serpent
sermon.why: Preached on Numbers 21:4-9 and John 3:12-17, Keller draws out exactly how the bronze serpent lifted up for a dying people foreshadows Christ lifted up on the cross, and what it means to look to Him in simple faith rather than earn healing by effort.

quiz.q: What provoked the LORD to send venomous snakes among the people in this chapter?

options.text: The people grew impatient and spoke against God and Moses, detesting the food He provided.
options.note: Verses 4 to 5 record this complaint directly before the snakes appear.

options.text: Israel's defeat in battle against the king of Arad.
options.note: That battle actually ended in Israel's victory at Hormah, not defeat, and is unrelated to the snakes.

options.text: Aaron's death on Mount Hor in the previous chapter.
options.note: Aaron's death is recorded earlier, but the snakes are tied specifically to this chapter's complaint against God's provision.

options.text: No reason is given for the snakes.
options.note: Verses 4 to 6 clearly connect the snakes to the people's complaint.

quiz.q: What did God instruct Moses to make so that the bitten people could be healed?

options.text: A bronze serpent mounted on a pole, to be looked at by anyone bitten.
options.note: Verses 8 to 9 describe this exact instruction and its fulfillment.

options.text: A special herbal remedy applied to the snakebites.
options.note: No remedy of this kind appears; the healing came through looking at the bronze serpent.

options.text: A tent set apart where the bitten could be quarantined.
options.note: The text describes no quarantine, only the command to look at the bronze serpent.

options.text: Nothing; the plague simply ran its course.
options.note: Verses 8 to 9 record a specific, God-given remedy.

quiz.q: How does Jesus Himself apply this bronze serpent scene, according to John 3:14-15?

options.text: He compares His own being lifted up on the cross to the serpent being lifted up, so that believers may have eternal life.
options.note: This is Jesus' own direct statement, making the typology explicit rather than merely inferred.

options.text: He uses it only as an illustration of Israel's past disobedience with no reference to Himself.
options.note: Jesus applies the image directly to His own crucifixion, not merely as a historical illustration.

options.text: He rejects the story as an unreliable legend.
options.note: Jesus treats the account as real history and draws a direct parallel to His own mission.

options.text: The Gospels never reference this passage.
options.note: John 3:14-15 references it explicitly.

quiz.q: What happened when Israel asked Sihon king of the Amorites for peaceful passage through his land?

options.text: Sihon refused, gathered his army, and attacked Israel, but Israel defeated him and took his land.
options.note: Verses 21 to 25 describe this refusal, battle, and outcome.

options.text: Sihon granted passage immediately without conflict.
options.note: The opposite happened; Sihon refused and attacked, leading to his defeat.

options.text: Israel bypassed Sihon's territory entirely without contact.
options.note: The text describes direct negotiation, refusal, and battle with Sihon, not avoidance.

options.text: The outcome of this encounter is not recorded.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 record Israel's victory and possession of the land in detail.

quiz.q: What later happened to the bronze serpent itself, according to 2 Kings 18:4?

options.text: King Hezekiah destroyed it because Israel had begun worshiping it as an idol.
options.note: 2 Kings 18:4 records Hezekiah's reform, breaking the bronze serpent, called Nehushtan, because it had become an object of worship.

options.text: It was placed permanently in the Most Holy Place as a treasured relic.
options.note: It was kept for a time but was ultimately destroyed once it became an idol, not enshrined as an ongoing object of reverence.

options.text: It was lost during Israel's conquest of Canaan.
options.note: It survived for centuries into the divided kingdom period, until Hezekiah's reform.

options.text: No later reference to it exists in Scripture.
options.note: 2 Kings 18:4 explicitly references it centuries later.

questions: Israel's impatience led them to despise the very provision God had given them daily. Where has restlessness tempted you to despise what God has already provided rather than trust Him for what is still ahead?
The people did not have to perform a cure, only look in faith at what God provided. Where are you still trying to earn healing or forgiveness instead of simply looking to Christ?

Jesus applied this very scene to His own crucifixion, being lifted up so that all who believe might live. How does knowing Jesus chose this image Himself deepen your confidence in what the cross accomplishes?
Centuries later, Israel turned this good gift into an idol, and it had to be destroyed. What good gifts in your own life are you tempted to worship instead of the God who gave them?
God judged real sin yet provided healing before the people even finished repenting. Where do you need to trust that God's grace can meet you as quickly as His judgment does?

translationNotes.note: Venomous snakes translates nachash saraph, literally fiery serpents, describing the burning pain of their venom rather than a literal fire; the same root later names the seraphim in Isaiah 6.
Bronze snake renders nechoshet, the same consonants as the Hebrew word for bronze, and the object is later called Nehushtan in 2 Kings 18:4, a name that plays on both bronze and serpent.

The Book of the Wars of the LORD, quoted in verses 14 to 15, is an otherwise unknown ancient source; its citation shows the biblical author drawing on existing records of Israel's early history rather than inventing the account.
